flashcards.front: What is a signal in signal processing?
flashcards.back: A signal is a function that conveys information about a phenomenon. It can be represented as a mathematical function of one or more independent variables (time, space, etc.).
A continuous-time signal is defined for every instant of time. It can take any value within a continuous range and is represented as x(t). Example: sin(t), e^(-t).
A discrete-time signal is defined only at specific instants of time. It is represented as x[n] where n is an integer, and is often derived by sampling a continuous-time signal.

flashcards.front: What is a Unit Step Signal u(t)?
flashcards.back: Unit step signal u(t) equals 0 for t<0 and 1 for t≥0. It is used to represent signals that start at t=0 and model switching operations.

flashcards.front: What is a Unit Impulse Signal δ(t)?
flashcards.back: Unit impulse (Dirac delta) has zero amplitude everywhere except at t=0 where it is infinite, with area (integral) equal to 1. Used to sample signal values and represent impulses.
Even signal: x(-t) = x(t), symmetric about y-axis. Odd signal: x(-t) = -x(t), anti-symmetric about origin. Any signal can be decomposed into even and odd components.
A signal is periodic if x(t + T) = x(t) for all t, where T is the fundamental period (smallest positive value). The fundamental frequency is f = 1/T and angular frequency ω = 2π/T.

flashcards.front: Define energy signal and power signal
flashcards.back: Energy signal: Total energy E is finite and non-zero, average power P = 0. Power signal: Average power P is finite and non-zero, energy E is infinite. A signal cannot be both.
Convolution combines two signals to produce a third signal: y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = ∫x(τ)h(t-τ)dτ. It represents how one signal modifies another in an LTI system.
Time-shifting x(t-t₀) shifts the signal right by t₀ if t₀>0, left if t₀<0. The amplitude remains unchanged; only the position changes.
Flipping or reversing x(t) gives x(-t), which reflects the signal about the y-axis (horizontal flip). It reverses the time axis.

flashcards.front: What is time scaling in signals?
flashcards.back: Time scaling compresses (a>1) or expands (a<1) the signal in time. x(at) compresses the signal by factor a. If a>1, signal appears narrower; if 0<a<1, it appears wider.
A system is linear if it satisfies superposition: Additivity T{x₁ + x₂} = T{x₁} + T{x₂} and Homogeneity T{ax} = aT{x}. Otherwise, it is non-linear.
A system is time-invariant if shifting input produces same shifted output. If x(t)→y(t), then x(t-t₀)→y(t-t₀). Otherwise, it is time-varying.
A causal system output depends only on present and past inputs, not future values. For impulse response h(t), h(t) = 0 for t<0. Also called physical or realizable system.
